content.text: WCAG Testing for Conversational UI Experiences

Conversational AI interfaces introduce accessibility challenges that standard web pages do not have: streamed text that updates in real time, thinking indicators that must be announced to screen readers, and a message history that grows dynamically. WCAG 2.1 AA compliance requires deliberate implementation of ARIA live regions, keyboard navigation through the full conversation flow, and sufficient colour contrast between user and AI messages.

This sub-subtopic covers the specific WCAG requirements that apply to chat UIs, how to automate accessibility scanning with axe-core and Playwright, how to test ARIA live region announcements, and how to structure a manual screen reader test plan.

attrs.alt: WCAG POUR principles applied to conversational AI UI and the automated test pipeline

content.text: ARIA Live Regions for Streaming Responses

Without a live region, a screen reader user types a message, hears nothing while the model generates, and has no indication the response arrived. The message container must be a live region so the screen reader announces new content automatically.
Automated Axe-Core Scan with Playwright

axe-core is the most widely used automated accessibility scanner. Run it in Playwright tests against the live chat UI — after the page loads, after a message is sent, and after the response streams in — to catch violations at each state transition.

# tests/accessibility/test_chat_a11y.py
import pytest
from playwright.sync_api import Page, expect
from axe_playwright_python.sync_playwright import Axe

axe = Axe()

BASE_URL = "http://localhost:5173"


@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def browser_context(playwright):
    browser = playwright.chromium.launch()
    context = browser.new_context()
    yield context
    context.close()
    browser.close()


@pytest.fixture
def page(browser_context):
    p = browser_context.new_page()
    yield p
    p.close()


def assert_no_axe_violations(page: Page, context_name: str):
    results = axe.run(page)
    violations = results.violations
    if violations:
        details = []
        for v in violations:
            nodes = "; ".join(n["html"][:80] for n in v.get("nodes", [])[:2])
            details.append(f"[{v['id']}] {v['description']}: {nodes}")
        pytest.fail(
            f"axe violations in '{context_name}':\n" + "\n".join(details)
        )


def test_chat_page_initial_load(page):
    page.goto(BASE_URL)
    page.wait_for_load_state("networkidle")
    assert_no_axe_violations(page, "initial load")


def test_chat_message_list_has_live_region(page):
    page.goto(BASE_URL)
    msg_list = page.locator('[role="log"][aria-live]')
    expect(msg_list).to_be_visible()
    assert msg_list.get_attribute("aria-live") == "polite"


def test_chat_input_has_label(page):
    page.goto(BASE_URL)
    # Input must have accessible name via label, aria-label, or aria-labelledby
    textarea = page.locator("textarea")
    label = textarea.evaluate(
        "el => el.labels?.[0]?.textContent || el.getAttribute('aria-label') || ''"
    )
    assert label.strip(), "Chat input has no accessible label"


def test_after_message_sent(page):
    page.goto(BASE_URL)
    page.fill("textarea", "What is the capital of France?")
    page.click('[aria-label="Send message"]')
    # Wait for response to appear in the log region
    page.wait_for_selector('[role="log"] .message--ai', timeout=15000)
    assert_no_axe_violations(page, "after AI response")


def test_thinking_indicator_has_role_status(page):
    page.goto(BASE_URL)
    page.fill("textarea", "Tell me a story.")
    page.click('[aria-label="Send message"]')
    # Check thinking indicator appears with correct role
    indicator = page.locator('[role="status"]')
    expect(indicator).to_be_visible()
    assert indicator.text_content().strip() != "", "Thinking indicator is empty"
Keyboard Navigation Test

Keyboard users must be able to complete the full chat flow — type a message, send it, read the response, and stop generation — without using a mouse. Test the full Tab order and ensure no keyboard traps exist.

# tests/accessibility/test_keyboard_nav.py
from playwright.sync_api import Page
import pytest


def test_full_keyboard_flow(page: Page):
    """Tab to input, type, Enter to send, Tab to messages, Tab to stop button."""
    page.goto("http://localhost:5173")

    # Focus should start on the chat input (or reach it via Tab)
    page.keyboard.press("Tab")
    focused = page.evaluate("document.activeElement.tagName")
    # Tab until we reach the textarea
    for _ in range(5):
        if page.evaluate("document.activeElement.tagName") == "TEXTAREA":
            break
        page.keyboard.press("Tab")

    assert page.evaluate("document.activeElement.tagName") == "TEXTAREA", (
        "Tab did not reach the chat input within 5 presses"
    )

    page.keyboard.type("What is machine learning?")
    page.keyboard.press("Enter")  # or Ctrl+Enter depending on UI

    # Thinking state: stop button should appear and be reachable
    stop_btn = page.locator('[aria-label="Stop generating response"]')
    stop_btn.wait_for(state="visible", timeout=5000)

    # Tab to stop button
    for _ in range(3):
        page.keyboard.press("Tab")
        if page.evaluate("document.activeElement.getAttribute('aria-label')") == "Stop generating response":
            break

    focused_label = page.evaluate("document.activeElement.getAttribute('aria-label')")
    assert focused_label == "Stop generating response", (
        f"Stop button not reachable via keyboard, focused: {focused_label}"
    )

    # Press Space to stop
    page.keyboard.press("Space")
    stop_btn.wait_for(state="hidden", timeout=3000)


def test_no_keyboard_trap(page: Page):
    """Tabbing past the last element should not trap focus in the chat widget."""
    page.goto("http://localhost:5173")
    # Press Tab many times and verify focus eventually leaves the chat area
    focus_chain = []
    for _ in range(20):
        page.keyboard.press("Tab")
        tag = page.evaluate("document.activeElement.tagName")
        role = page.evaluate("document.activeElement.getAttribute('role') or ''")
        focus_chain.append(f"{tag}[{role}]")

    # Focus should have cycled through multiple elements — if it's stuck on one, it's a trap
    unique = set(focus_chain)
    assert len(unique) > 2, f"Possible keyboard trap — only {unique} in Tab chain"
Colour Contrast and Streaming State Visibility

Chat bubbles must meet WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text. AI and user messages distinguished only by colour (e.g. blue vs grey bubbles) fail WCAG 1.4.1 — add a visual label or avatar.
Manual Screen Reader Test Plan
Test Case
Steps
Pass Criteria
New message announced
Enable NVDA/VoiceOver. Send a message. Wait for AI response.
Screen reader reads new AI message without user action
Thinking state announced
Send message. Listen during generation.
Reader announces 'AI is thinking' or similar when generation starts
Input field labelled
Navigate to input with screen reader.
Reader announces label (e.g. 'Message, text area') not just placeholder
Stop button accessible
During streaming, navigate with Tab.
Stop button announced with clear label; activatable with Space/Enter
Message history navigable
After 5+ messages, navigate up through history.
Reader can move between messages; each identifies sender
Error messages accessible
Trigger an error (disconnect). Navigate to error.
Error message announced via live region or focus moved to alert
CI Integration with axe-core and Lighthouse

Run automated accessibility scans in CI on every PR that touches frontend code. Lighthouse's accessibility audit and axe-core together cover different WCAG criterion subsets — run both for better coverage.

# .github/workflows/accessibility.yml
name: Accessibility Tests

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - "src/frontend/**"
      - "src/components/**"

jobs:
  a11y-axe:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: "20" }

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          npm ci
          pip install pytest playwright axe-playwright-python
          playwright install chromium

      - name: Start dev server
        run: npm run dev &
        env: { PORT: "5173" }

      - name: Wait for server
        run: |
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            curl -s http://localhost:5173 > /dev/null && break
            sleep 1
          done

      - name: Run axe-core accessibility tests
        run: pytest tests/accessibility/ -v --tb=short

      - name: Lighthouse accessibility audit
        uses: treosh/lighthouse-ci-action@v11
        with:
          urls: "http://localhost:5173"
          uploadArtifacts: true
          temporaryPublicStorage: true
          configPath: ".lighthouserc.json"

  a11y-report:
    needs: a11y-axe
    if: failure()
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Comment on PR
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: '❌ **Accessibility tests failed.** Check the [axe-core results](${{ github.server_url }}/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}) for WCAG violations.'
            })
Accessible Error Handling for AI Failures

When the LLM call fails — network error, rate limit, content filter — the error must be communicated to all users, including screen reader users. A toast notification that disappears after 3 seconds is inaccessible if the user was focused elsewhere.

Unable to generate response

The AI service is temporarily unavailable. Please try again in a moment.
Handling Streamed Content and Focus Management

Streamed AI responses that auto-scroll the page can steal keyboard focus and confuse screen reader users who are navigating earlier messages. Implement a scroll strategy that only follows the stream if the user was already at the bottom.
Never call .focus() on the message list or individual messages after streaming — this breaks keyboard users who are reading earlier content.
Set aria-live='off' temporarily if appending many chunks rapidly would produce too many announcements; set it back to 'polite' when done.
For very long responses, consider announcing only the first sentence via the live region to avoid overwhelming screen reader users.
Summary
WCAG testing for conversational AI UIs focuses on four areas unique to the format: live regions (

marks.type: for the thinking indicator), keyboard navigation through the full send → stream → stop flow, colour contrast on chat bubbles, and screen reader announcement of each new AI message.

content.text: Automate steps 1–4 (axe-core scan, live region assertion, keyboard nav test, contrast check) in CI using Playwright. Run manual NVDA/VoiceOver tests against the full conversation flow before each release, using the test plan above as a checklist. axe-core catches roughly 57% of WCAG issues automatically; the remainder require a human tester with a screen reader.
